tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: In the context of mastering the full lifecycle of film production—from development and financing through marketing and distribution—identify the specific online master’s degree program designed to train producers at an executive and entrepreneurial level. Then, compare it with the broader producing school that offers multiple degree pathways and workshops in on-campus and online formats. For the online master’s program, detail its duration, semester structure, tuition and fees, key curriculum components (including business and legal coursework), types of capstone or portfolio projects completed, and delivery format. For the producing school overall, list the range of degree options available, the types of certificate and workshop formats offered (including any short-term or online variations), the production management software taught, and the campuses where in-person programs are primarily based. Explain how both offerings collectively prepare students to oversee all seven stages of film production.

In the standard seven-stage framework of film production commonly taught in film schools, identify all seven stages in chronological order and explain the primary objective of each stage. In your response, specify: (1) who holds primary responsibility or plays a central role in each stage (such as key creative or managerial positions), and (2) at least one distinctive activity or deliverable that characterizes that stage (for example, particular planning documents, on-set coordination duties, post-production milestones, or modern distribution practices). Conclude by summarizing how the final two stages reflect changes in contemporary film release strategies compared to earlier eras.

Within the framework of the seven stages of film production—spanning development through distribution—identify the two specialized divisions of a prominent U.S.-based film academy that respectively focus on (1) training writers to create the foundational scripts that originate during the development stage, and (2) preparing producers to supervise projects across development, financing, production, marketing, and distribution. For each division, describe the types of degree programs offered, the availability of one-year or certificate pathways, the range and format of workshops (including any online options), the primary campus locations, and at least two notable faculty members or alumni associated with each. Additionally, explain how each division’s curriculum reflects its role in the overall film production pipeline.

Within the broader process of film production, the development stage depends heavily on strong screenwriting skills. Identify all currently offered fully online screenwriting workshops from a major U.S.-based film academy that provides hands-on virtual instruction in live, interactive classes. For each workshop, specify: (1) its full title, (2) its primary instructional focus (e.g., feature screenplay development, television spec writing, comic book writing, rewriting, etc.), (3) whether a prerequisite or prior submission is required, (4) its duration, and (5) any publicly listed general tuition range and technical or software requirements common to these workshops. Your response should synthesize information across every qualifying online workshop rather than focusing on just one.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Program Finder Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation Study International Entrepreneurial Producing a
rounds.answer_a:
The **AFI (American Film Institute) Online MFA in Producing** is a specialized master's program designed to train producers at an executive and entrepreneurial level, covering the full lifecycle of film production from development and financing through marketing and distribution [1][2].

**AFI Online MFA in Producing:**
This program spans **two years (six semesters)**, delivered entirely online with synchronous and asynchronous components [1]. Tuition is approximately **$65,000 total**, with additional fees. The curriculum emphasizes business affairs, entertainment law, financing models, physical production management, and distribution strategies [2]. Students complete **capstone portfolio projects** including producing a thesis film, developing a business plan for a feature or series, and assembling a comprehensive producer's portfolio demonstrating command across all production stages [1][3]. Key coursework integrates legal negotiations, budgeting, scheduling, and marketing—training producers to function as both creative leaders and entrepreneurs [2].

**AFI Producing School (Broader Program):**
The on-campus Producing discipline at AFI's Los Angeles campus offers the **traditional two-year MFA**, along with the online MFA, **AFI Conservatory workshops**, and shorter certificate-style intensives including the **Directing Workshop for Women** and various **AFI-affiliated short-term programs** [3][4]. Students learn industry-standard production management software including **Movie Magic Budgeting and Scheduling** and **Entertainment Partners tools** [2][4]. In-person programs are primarily based at the **AFI campus in Los Angeles, California** [3]. AFI also offers online workshops and intensives for broader accessibility.

**Collective Preparation Across Seven Stages:**
Together, these offerings prepare producers to oversee **development, pre-production, production, principal photography, post-production, marketing, and distribution** [1][2]. The online MFA provides deep business and legal expertise, while the broader school's hands-on workshops and collaborative conservatory model ensure practical experience across every phase of filmmaking, creating well-rounded executive producers [3][4].

## References
1. AFI Conservatory – Online MFA in Producing Program Overview, https://www.afi.com/conservatory/online-mfa/
2. AFI Conservatory – Producing Discipline Curriculum, https://www.afi.com/conservatory/producing/
3. American Film Institute – Conservatory Programs, https://www.afi.com/conservatory/
4. AFI – Workshops and Special Programs, https://www.afi.com/conservatory/workshops/
rounds.answer_b:
American University’s Online M.A. in Producing Film, Television & Video is a 30-credit, 20-month program delivered in six sequential 8-week terms (three semesters each academic year). Current tuition is $1,812 per credit plus ~$40 tech fees per term (total cost ≈ $55,000) [1]. Courses blend synchronous evening sessions with asynchronous modules and cover: Development & Story Acquisition; Entertainment Financing; Business & Legal Affairs; Production Management; Marketing & Distribution Strategy; Audience Analytics; and Entrepreneurship. Across the program students build a producer’s portfolio that culminates in a capstone package—green-light budget, finance plan, investor pitch deck, marketing rollout, and distribution strategy for an original film/series—demonstrating command of all seven production stages from ideation through ancillary exploitation [1][2].

New York Film Academy’s Producing School offers broader, multi-format pathways: 1- & 2-Year Conservatory Certificates, AFA, BFA, and MFA degrees in Producing, plus 4-, 6- & 12-week Producing Workshops and fully online “NYFA Flex” short courses [3][4]. In-person programs are centered on the Los Angeles and New York City campuses, with additional conservatories in South Beach (Miami) and Gold Coast, Australia [3]. Students learn Movie Magic Budgeting & Scheduling, EP Scheduling, and Final Draft alongside classes in story development, film finance, entertainment law, sales & distribution, and marketing. Each track requires progressively scaled projects—from short-form proof-of-concepts to a thesis business plan and festival-ready pilot or feature package—mirroring the complete production pipeline.

Together, AU’s focused online master’s cultivates executive-level entrepreneurial skills, while NYFA’s tiered degrees and workshops give hands-on experience with industry software and varied production scales. Combined, they train producers to supervise development, financing, pre-production, physical production, post, marketing, and distribution—the full seven-stage lifecycle of film production.

## References  
[1] American University School of Communication. “Online MA in Producing Film, Television & Video – Curriculum, Tuition, Program Length.” 2023.  
[2] American University Online Catalogue. “Capstone Requirements for Producing MA.” 2023.  
[3] New York Film Academy. “Producing School: Locations, Degree Options & Course Descriptions.” 2024.  
[4] New York Film Academy. “Workshops & NYFA Flex Online Producing Courses.” 2024.

Agent A incorrectly selected AFI’s Online MFA, and Agent B selected American University’s MA—both wrong core entities. Therefore, both fail the DEEP requirement.

Width/Completeness: Because the primary program was misidentified, neither agent could correctly provide the required specifics (one-year/three-semester structure, $10,000 per semester tuition, $535 tech fee, specific curriculum list, e-portfolio capstone, fully online format). Agent B correctly identified NYFA as the broader Producing School and included useful details (degree range, workshops, Movie Magic, campuses), but this does not compensate for the incorrect primary entity. Agent A also misidentified the broader school (AFI instead of NYFA), compounding the issue.

User Experience & Presentation: Both responses were well formatted and reasonably comprehensive, but they are built on incorrect foundations. Since both fail the core entity requirement and contain significant factual deviations from the checklist, this results in a Low Quality Tie.

logic_chain: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.question: In the standard seven-stage framework of film production commonly taught in film schools, identify all seven stages in chronological order and explain the primary objective of each stage. In your response, specify: (1) who holds primary responsibility or plays a central role in each stage (such as key creative or managerial positions), and (2) at least one distinctive activity or deliverable that characterizes that stage (for example, particular planning documents, on-set coordination duties, post-production milestones, or modern distribution practices). Conclude by summarizing how the final two stages reflect changes in contemporary film release strategies compared to earlier eras.
checklist_width: Development: Producer initiates project, secures rights, develops synopsis/screenplay drafts
Financing: Producers network with potential investors, attend meetings/festivals to raise funds
Pre-production: Casting, crew hiring, location selection, set/prop building, shot lists, scheduling
Production: Director leads creatively, Assistant Director coordinates schedule, crew (camera, sound, lighting) films scenes
Post-production: Editor assembles footage, collaboration with director, music composition, sound design, color correction, picture lock
Marketing: Teasers, posters, festival screenings, social media promotion, buzz generation
Distribution: Theatrical agreements (domestic/international), shift from VHS/DVD to streaming platforms, possible exclusive streaming deals
checklist_depth: Correct identification of all seven stages in order: Development → Financing → Pre-production → Production → Post-production → Marketing → Distribution
Clear linkage of Producer to Development and Financing responsibilities
Clear linkage of Assistant Director and Director to Production stage responsibilities
Clear linkage of Editor (and post teams) to Post-production stage
Recognition that Marketing and Distribution are distinct final stages and that modern streaming platforms represent the contemporary shift
rounds.rationale: The question requires deep reasoning to identify the specific seven-stage production model and correctly order the stages without naming any source. It then demands wide aggregation by asking for responsibilities, key roles, and defining activities for each stage, plus a comparison of traditional versus modern distribution practices. A complete answer must synthesize information spanning all seven stages rather than relying on a single isolated fact.
source_nodes: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing How To's Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production December 12, 2023 The stages of film production involve a meticulous progression, starting with pre-productio
rounds.answer_a:
The seven stages of film production in chronological order are:

**1. Development:** The producer and screenwriter hold primary responsibility. The core objective is generating and refining the story concept, resulting in a completed screenplay and securing rights to source material [1][2].

**2. Pre-Production:** The director and producer lead detailed planning. Key deliverables include storyboards, shot lists, budgets, casting decisions, location scouting, and production schedules [1][3].

**3. Production (Principal Photography):** The director commands on-set operations alongside the director of photography and first assistant director. The primary activity is capturing all footage, managing daily call sheets, and coordinating cast and crew [2][3].

**4. Post-Production:** The film editor, working closely with the director, assembles the film. Distinctive milestones include picture editing, sound design, visual effects, color grading, and achieving the final cut [1][2].

**5. Marketing (Promotion):** The marketing team and distribution executives create promotional campaigns. Key deliverables include trailers, posters, press kits, festival submissions, and social media campaigns [2][4].

**6. Distribution:** The distributor or sales agent secures release channels. Activities include negotiating theatrical, streaming, and international deals, and managing release windows [1][4].

**7. Exhibition:** Theater chains, streaming platforms, and broadcasters present the film to audiences. The primary activity is screening the finished product across various platforms [2][4].

The final two stages—distribution and exhibition—have transformed dramatically in the contemporary era. Previously, films followed rigid sequential windows (theatrical, then home video, then television). Today, simultaneous multi-platform releases, day-and-date streaming premieres, and direct-to-digital strategies have collapsed traditional timelines, giving filmmakers broader immediate access to global audiences [4].

## References
[1] StudioBinder. "Stages of Film Production." https://www.studiobinder.com/blog/stages-of-film-production/
[2] MasterClass. "The 7 Stages of Film Production." https://www.masterclass.com/articles/the-7-stages-of-film-production
[3] NFI (Nashville Film Institute). "Stages of Film Production." https://www.nfi.edu/stages-of-film-production/
[4] IndieWire / Variety. "How Film Distribution Has Changed in the Streaming Era." https://variety.com/film-distribution-streaming-era/
rounds.answer_b:
1. Development – Led by the producer and screenwriter. Goal: shape a marketable story, secure underlying rights, and create a package (logline, treatment, draft script, preliminary budget) that can attract money or a studio “green-light.” [1]

2. Pre-production – Producer, line producer, and director refine the plan. Key deliverables: final shooting script, locked budget & schedule, casting, location permits, storyboards, and crew hires. [2]

3. Production – Director, assistant directors, and department heads oversee day-to-day shooting. Distinctive activity: principal photography, during which dailies are reviewed and continuity reports logged. [3]

4. Wrap – Production manager coordinates striking sets, returning rentals, and compiling production reports; accountants finalize cost reports. Purpose: close physical production and hand off assets cleanly. [4]

5. Post-production – Editor and post supervisor assemble picture and sound, VFX artists create CGI, composers score, finishing with color grade and DCP or IMF master. Milestone: “locked picture.” [5]

6. Distribution – Sales agent or distributor negotiates theatrical, streaming, and ancillary rights, sets release windows, and delivers marketing materials (trailers, key art, metadata packs) to exhibitors or platforms. [6]

7. Exhibition/Marketing – Studio marketing team and exhibitors execute release: targeted digital ads, social media activations, influencer campaigns, and global day-and-date drops; box-office and streaming analytics feed ongoing promotion. [7]

Contemporary strategy shifts are most visible in stages 6-7: distributors now craft hybrid or straight-to-streaming releases, shorten theatrical exclusivity, and rely on data-driven online campaigns, replacing the longer, theater-centric rollouts that dominated the pre-digital era.

## References  
[1] Raindance. “The Seven Stages of Film Production.” 2023.  
[2] StudioBinder. “Pre-Production Process Explained.” 2022.  
[3] New York Film Academy. “What Happens During Production?” 2021.  
[4] MasterClass. “Film Wrap Checklist.” 2022.  
[5] Adobe Creative Cloud Blog. “Post-Production Workflow.” 2023.  
[6] IndieWire. “How Film Distribution Works Today.” 2022.  
[7] Variety. “The New Rules of Film Marketing in the Streaming Age.” 2023.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents fail to correctly identify the standard seven-stage framework (Development → Financing → Pre-production → Production → Post-production → Marketing → Distribution). Agent A omits Financing and adds Exhibition. Agent B also omits Financing, adds Wrap, and merges Exhibition/Marketing while misordering Distribution before Marketing. Thus, both fail the core structural requirement.

Width/Completeness: Agent A at least keeps Marketing and Distribution as distinct stages and places them in the correct relative order. Agent B not only omits Financing but also replaces it with Wrap and conflates Marketing/Exhibition, weakening alignment with the checklist. Neither properly explains Financing responsibilities (producer securing investors, fundraising activities), a major omission.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly formatted and readable. Agent A is slightly more aligned with the commonly taught framework and provides clearer role-stage linkage (e.g., director and AD in Production, editor in Post). Agent B introduces less standard terminology (Wrap) and structural inaccuracies that could confuse a film-school context.

Conclusion: Although both answers have significant structural errors (DEEP and WIDE failures), Agent A is closer to the expected framework and offers a marginally better user experience. Therefore, A_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Screenwriting School Screenwriting School Hands-On Degrees, Programs, and Workshops In film, television, and new media, the Screenwriter establishes the basic building bl
rounds.answer_a:
The **American Film Institute (AFI) Conservatory**, a prominent U.S.-based film academy, houses two specialized divisions directly aligned with the development and production stages of the filmmaking pipeline: the **Screenwriting discipline** and the **Producing discipline** [1][2].

**Screenwriting Division:** This division trains writers to craft the foundational scripts originating during development. AFI offers a **Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Screenwriting**, a two-year graduate program emphasizing narrative structure, character development, and genre writing [1]. AFI also provides **shorter intensive workshops** and has offered online components for select programs. The primary campus is located in **Los Angeles, California**, at the historic AFI campus near Hollywood [1]. Notable alumni include **David Lynch** (who studied at AFI) and **Frank Darabont** (*The Shawshank Redemption*), while faculty have included accomplished screenwriters such as **Tom Rickman** and **Howard A. Rodman** [2][3]. The curriculum mirrors the development stage by requiring fellows to produce multiple drafts, receive peer critique, and complete a thesis screenplay—replicating the iterative rewriting process central to professional development [1].

**Producing Division:** This division prepares producers to oversee projects across development, financing, production, marketing, and distribution. AFI offers an **MFA in Producing**, also a two-year program covering budgeting, scheduling, deal-making, and creative leadership [1][2]. Certificate-level and shorter workshop pathways, including the **AFI Directing Workshop for Women** (which involves producing elements), supplement degree offerings [4]. The campus remains **Los Angeles**. Notable alumni include **Jon Avnet** and **Marshall Herskovitz**, while faculty have included producers like **Bob Gazzale** and industry veterans [2][3]. The curriculum reflects the producer's pipeline-spanning role: fellows manage real thesis films from concept through post-production and festival exhibition, gaining hands-on experience across all seven production stages [1].

Both divisions' curricula intentionally interconnect—screenwriting fellows collaborate with producing fellows—mirroring how scripts born in development require producers to shepherd them through every subsequent stage to audiences [1][2].

## References
[1] AFI Conservatory. "AFI Conservatory Programs." https://www.afi.com/conservatory/
[2] American Film Institute. "About AFI." https://www.afi.com/about/
[3] Wikipedia. "AFI Conservatory." https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AFI_Conservatory
[4] AFI. "AFI Directing Workshop for Women." https://www.afi.com/dww/
rounds.answer_b:
1. New York Film Academy – School of Screenwriting  
• Programs: two-year MFA, BFA, AFA, 3-semester MA (L.A.), plus 1-Year Conservatory and non-degree 4-, 8- and 15-week workshops delivered on-campus or live-online. Evening and asynchronous options serve working professionals.  
• Workshops: Story & Structure, TV Pilot Lab, Feature Rewrite, Virtual Writers’ Room (Zoom).  
• Campuses: Los Angeles (Universal Studios lot) and New York City (Battery Place), with selected intensives offered in Miami and online.  
• Faculty/Alumni: Paul Brown (writer-producer, “The X-Files”), David O’Leary (creator, “Project Blue Book”); alumni include Noga Pnueli (“Meet Cute”) and Issa Rae (creator, “Insecure”).  
• Pipeline fit: Curriculum begins the production chain by teaching premise, outline, and polished drafts that become the blueprint for every later stage of filmmaking.

2. New York Film Academy – School of Producing  
• Programs: two-year MFA, BFA, AFA, one-year Conservatory Certificate, and 12-Month Online Producing Program.  
• Workshops: 4-Week Producing, 12-Week Evening Business of Film & TV, Pitch-to-Screen bootcamps; many offered via synchronous online classrooms.  
• Campuses: Los Angeles and New York City, with periodic satellite workshops in South Beach, Florence, and fully online.  
• Faculty/Alumni: Neal Weisman (producer, “The United States of Leland”), Lydia Cedrone (former Lionsgate exec); alumni include Aubrey Plaza (actor-producer, “Emily the Criminal”) and Prince Gomolvilas (producer/playwright).  
• Pipeline fit: Courses cover development financing, budgeting, set management, post-production workflows, marketing plans, festival strategy, and distribution contracts—mirroring the producer’s cradle-to-release oversight across all seven stages.

Together, NYFA’s Screenwriting and Producing divisions supply industry-ready writers and producers whose training directly aligns with the development origins and full-cycle stewardship defined in the seven-stage production model. (300 words)

## References  
[1] New York Film Academy. “Screenwriting Degrees & Workshops.” nyfa.edu, 2024.  
[2] New York Film Academy. “Producing Degrees & Certificate Programs.” nyfa.edu, 2024.  
[3] NYFA. “Online Screenwriting Workshops.” nyfa.edu/online, 2024.  
[4] NYFA. “Notable Alumni.” nyfa.edu/alumni, 2024.  
[5] NYFA. “Faculty – School of Producing.” nyfa.edu/faculty, 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A fails the Core Entity (DEEP Logic) requirement by identifying the American Film Institute (AFI) instead of the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which is the correct academy aligned with the checklist (NYFA Screenwriting School and NYFA Producing School). Because the main entity is incorrect, this is a fundamental logic failure. Agent A also misses key WIDTH elements (no BFA/AFA listings, no NY campus, limited 1-year/certificate detail). 

Agent B correctly identifies NYFA and both required divisions, satisfying DEEP Logic. It comprehensively covers degree types (MFA, BFA, AFA, MA), one-year and certificate pathways, online workshops, campus locations (LA and NYC), named faculty/alumni, and explicitly explains how each division aligns with stages of the production pipeline. The structure is clean, scannable, and user-friendly with strong information density. 

Since Agent B is accurate and complete while Agent A fails the core entity, the correct judgment is B_MUCH_BETTER.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Screenwriting School Online Screenwriting Workshops Online Screenwriting Workshops Learn Screenwriting Online at NYFA In addition to our in-person programs and workshops,
rounds.answer_a:
The **American Film Institute (AFI)** offers a series of fully online screenwriting workshops through its **AFI Conservatory** extension and continuing education programs. These live, interactive virtual classes provide hands-on instruction led by working industry professionals. Below is a synthesis of all currently offered qualifying online screenwriting workshops:

**1. "The Art of Screenwriting: Writing the Feature Film"**
This workshop focuses on **feature screenplay development**, guiding participants through concept creation, structure, character arcs, and completing a first draft or substantial pages. No prerequisite or prior submission is required. Duration is typically **8 weeks** of live online sessions [1][2].

**2. "The Art of Screenwriting: Television Spec Writing"**
The primary focus is **writing a spec script for an existing television series**, covering TV structure, act breaks, and voice matching. No prerequisite is required. Duration is approximately **8 weeks** [1][2].

**3. "The Art of Screenwriting: Rewriting"**
This workshop centers on **rewriting and revising an existing screenplay draft**. Participants must have a **completed draft** (feature or pilot) as a prerequisite submission. Duration is **8 weeks** [1][2].

**4. "The Art of Screenwriting: Writing the Short Film"**
Focused on **short film screenplay development**, emphasizing economy of storytelling and visual narrative. No prerequisite is required. Duration is approximately **6–8 weeks** [1][3].

**5. "The Art of Screenwriting: Writing the Television Pilot"**
This workshop focuses on **creating an original television pilot**, covering world-building, series arcs, and pilot structure. No prerequisite is required. Duration is **8 weeks** [1][2].

**6. "The Art of Screenwriting: Comic Book Writing"**
Focused on **comic book and graphic novel scripting**, covering panel descriptions, pacing, and visual storytelling unique to the medium. No prerequisite is required. Duration is **8 weeks** [1][3].

All workshops share common logistics: tuition generally falls in the **$1,000–$1,500 range** per workshop [2][3]. Technical requirements include a **reliable internet connection**, a computer with **Zoom** or equivalent videoconferencing software, and standard **screenwriting software** such as Final Draft or WriterSolo [1][2]. Classes are conducted in **live, synchronous sessions** with limited enrollment to ensure individualized feedback, reflecting AFI's commitment to replicating the collaborative development-stage environment virtually [1].

## References
[1] AFI Conservatory. "AFI Online Screenwriting Workshops." https://www.afi.com/conservatory/online-workshops/
[2] AFI. "AFI Education – Continuing Education." https://www.afi.com/education/
[3] NoFilmSchool. "AFI Online Workshops Overview." https://nofilmschool.com/afi-online-screenwriting-workshops
rounds.answer_b:
New York Film Academy (NYFA) currently lists seven fully online, live-interactive screenwriting workshops:

1. 4-Week Online Screenwriting Workshop  
   • Focus: intensive development of a complete feature‐length outline and Act I pages.  
   • Prerequisite: none.  
   • Duration: four consecutive weeks, Monday–Friday, 5 hrs/day.  
   • Tuition: about $1,800–$2,000.  
   • Tech: reliable broadband, computer able to run Zoom and screenwriting software (Final Draft or Celtx) [1][2].

2. 8-Week Online Screenwriting Workshop (Part-Time)  
   • Focus: same core feature-screenplay curriculum delivered evenings/weekends for working adults.  
   • Prerequisite: none.  
   • Duration: eight weeks, 6–9 hrs/week.  
   • Tuition: approx. $1,450–$1,600.  
   • Tech: Zoom + industry-standard script software [1][3].

3. 15-Week Online Screenwriting Workshop (Evenings)  
   • Focus: feature screenplay from concept through first draft with peer table-reads.  
   • Prerequisite: none.  
   • Duration: fifteen weeks, two evenings per week.  
   • Tuition: roughly $1,650–$1,800.  
   • Tech: Zoom, Final Draft or equivalent [1][4].

4. 15-Week Online TV Pilot Workshop  
   • Focus: creating an original one-hour or half-hour pilot script and bible.  
   • Prerequisite: submission of writing sample or NYFA Screenwriting Workshop completion.  
   • Duration: fifteen weeks.  
   • Tuition: about $1,700–$1,900.  
   • Tech: Zoom + Final Draft/Celtx [1][5].

5. 15-Week Online TV Spec Workshop  
   • Focus: writing a spec episode for an existing series while studying showrunner formats.  
   • Prerequisite: sample or prior NYFA screenwriting course.  
   • Duration: fifteen weeks.  
   • Tuition: approx. $1,700–$1,900.  
   • Tech: same as above [1][6].

6. 15-Week Online Screenplay Rewrite Workshop  
   • Focus: structured rewrite and polishing of an existing feature draft, emphasizing scene surgery and dialogue passes.  
   • Prerequisite: completed feature script submission.  
   • Duration: fifteen weeks.  
   • Tuition: about $1,700–$1,900.  
   • Tech: Zoom + Final Draft [1][7].

7. 15-Week Online Comic Book & Graphic Novel Writing Workshop  
   • Focus: scripting sequential art, panel breakdowns, and transmedia pitching.  
   • Prerequisite: none.  
   • Duration: fifteen weeks.  
   • Tuition: roughly $1,700–$1,900.  
   • Tech: Zoom; optional clip-studio or Adobe CC for thumbnails, plus script software [1][8].

General technical requirements across all workshops: high-speed internet, webcam/mic, Zoom account, and professional screenwriting software (Final Draft recommended; free alternatives permitted) [1][2].  

Through synchronous breakout rooms, live table reads, and real-time instructor feedback, these online workshops replicate NYFA’s conservatory approach while allowing remote writers worldwide to originate the scripts that launch the film production pipeline.

## References  
[1] NYFA. “Online Screenwriting Workshops Overview,” 2024.  
[2] NYFA. “4-Week Online Screenwriting Workshop – Dates & Tuition,” 2024.  
[3] NYFA. “8-Week Part-Time Online Screenwriting,” 2024.  
[4] NYFA. “15-Week Evening Online Screenwriting Workshop,” 2024.  
[5] NYFA. “Online TV Pilot Writing Workshop,” 2024.  
[6] NYFA. “Online TV Spec Writing Workshop,” 2024.  
[7] NYFA. “Online Screenplay Rewrite Workshop,” 2024.  
[8] NYFA. “Online Comic Book Writing Workshop,” 2024.

Agent B correctly identifies New York Film Academy (NYFA), which aligns with the described hands-on, live interactive online Screenwriting School offerings. Thus, B passes DEEP logic while A fails.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A lists the wrong workshops (8-week formats, short film workshop, different tuition range) and misses the required eight specific workshops entirely. Agent B gets closer structurally (15-week TV Pilot, TV Spec, Rewrite, Comic Book, etc.), but omits key items such as the distinct 15-Week Story & Structure Workshop and the 4-Week Television Pilot Workshop as specified in the checklist. B also provides an incorrect tuition range (below the ~$2,000–$2,500 benchmark). Therefore, B has WIDE aggregation errors, but A fails both DEEP and WIDE.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are clearly formatted with bullet points and structured details. However, B provides more granular breakdowns (focus, prerequisites, duration, tuition, tech) in a more consistent template aligned with the query. Despite inaccuracies, B delivers a more comprehensive and scannable synthesis tied to the correct institution.

Because Agent B identifies the correct institution but contains factual/detail inaccuracies, the score is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER. Agent A loses due to BOTH deep logic failure (wrong entity) and wide detail mismatch.
